This week, we're going to set up your own and just upload the artifacts into your workspace so that we can start just like generating content quickly.
So this is like I said, this is going to.
This is just a test project, and this is what we call the agentic pipeline.
The reason we call it agentic is because it goes back and grades its own work, and then if it didn't do a good job, it's going to fix the mistakes or any things that it didn't take into account.
So the input, for example, for the input, we will use a topic.
This can be a keyword or it can be a full title, and we give it an assignment direction.
This is kind of an article brief that covers the title, like the primary persona, and we, like the outline is the most important part of the article.
So we will spend a lot of time manually researching like the top ranking content.
And collecting information from different sources in order to make sure that the outline covers everything it needs to cover to rank well and also that the structure is on point.
One thing that, for example, with AI is that if you don't give it a good enough outline or if you don't tell it what you're expecting of the outline, the outline can be very much all over the place.
And it's kind of like repeating things in different sections.
The structure is hard to follow.
And this is super important for like just not just SEO, but also for AI visibility.
LLMs use chunking, so they will extract different parts of an article to answer a specific question.
And if the structure doesn't work, if the structure is kind of like incoherent, it's going to be less likely to be cited by an LLM just because it's just not easy to follow.
So we will provide the outline.

And then we will run our researcher, who, which is, right now we're using Tavoli and EXA, so we used to use Perplexity, but we found that EXA and Tavoli are much more advanced in terms of research quality, and they're able to find more higher quality sources than Perplexity.

So the research step is going to take the outline that we provided, and it's going to ask questions, okay, what are the questions that need to be answered in order to generate an article that covers this topic?
And it's going to, like, generate a research document with all of the information that the workflow can reference while writing the article.
This allows us to generate articles that are comprehensive, they're not, like, super surface level, and they reference high quality sources.
As opposed to kind of like random blog posts.
And this is especially important in like highly regulated industries.
And then what it will do is it will grade the quality of the research.
So it will, after it generates the research document, it's going to analyze the answers to see if the research that was found actually answers the question.
And if it did not answer a question, it's going to go back and try to find additional sources of information to fill that gap.
So it's kind of like trying to ensure that all of the information that is provided is as accurate and as up to date for the content that we're trying to generate.
Once it, once the research is done, and as you can see, kind of like the research, different research steps.
like the research takes 11 minutes versus the.
Article Draft, which is like five minutes.

So the Article Draft pulls the writing guidelines from the artifacts, whereas the company context and the audience personas are pulled at the very beginning, and they influence also the angle and like who we're talking to and what are the pain points that the content is going to mention.
And at this stage, we will add internal links.
We'll do like a run through to make sure that the structure looks well, that the AI did not change the outline for some reason.
And then we add the internal link and also the source links to different claims.
And then this is a very important step from a quality perspective, which is to validate the writing guidelines.
So it's going to use the writing guidelines and also the we have a proofreader checklist and it's going to grade the draft against those writing guidelines.
And if it notices any paragraphs or sentences where it does not follow the writing guidelines or the checklist, it's going to go back in and fix it.
So, for example, it's going to give a confidence score on a scale of 0 to 1 for how closely it followed the writing guidelines.
And it's also going to provide a change of summary.
For example, like it's going to fix like a structural issue or, you know, if we say don't use a specific sentence structure, like it's not X, it's Y, which is something that AI loves to use.
It might, those structures might slip through, but they will be like detected at this step.
The thing with writing guidelines and AI is that we need to strike the right balance between providing enough instruction, but not going overboard so as to confuse the AI.
But there's always going to be...
There some variation in terms of what are the instructions that it follows versus the instructions that it ignores.
Sometimes without being aware of it, we might be providing contradicting instructions to AI.
So by incorporating this step, we kind of allow the AI to go back and kind of double check if it actually followed the instructions that we provided.
So it's just like an extra layer of protection to make sure that we don't have to do a whole lot of manual revising later.
And then once we validate the writing guidelines, that's when we will do a more in-depth like manual review.
So an editor is going to read the article, double check all of the links that it's added, like double check claims.
If we're mentioning specific stats or research reports, we always double check those to make sure that we're
We're providing accurate information.
And then the last step in this process is to generate cover images, which we actually have a designer in-house who uses any brand guidelines that you have on visual assets.
